items.q: How many semitones are in a perfect fifth?
items.why: Students count fifth as 'five steps' but the name refers to letter NAMES (1-2-3-4-5), not semitones. C to G spans 5 letter names but 7 semitones (C-C#-D-D#-E-F-F#-G).

items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.q: Which orchestral family does the saxophone belong to?
items.misconception: Brass (because it is made of brass).
items.why: Students classify by material. Instrument families are defined by SOUND PRODUCTION: woodwinds use a reed or air across an edge. The sax uses a reed, so despite the brass body, it's woodwind.

items.q: Who developed the symphonic poem as a one-movement orchestral form?
items.why: Correct answer: Liszt. “Berlioz” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is music without an extra-musical narrative called?
items.why: Correct answer: Absolute music. “Programme music” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Berlioz premiere his programmatic 'Symphonie fantastique'?
items.why: Exact answer: 1830. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
